title: Backtracking — ROI & Pattern
oneSentence: Explore all paths, choose an option, explore deeper, then backtrack to try the next option.
description: Master the Backtracking template twice. First through guided line-by-line tracing of Subsets, then by reconstructing the core steps.

roi.complexity: Time: O(2^N) or O(N!) | Space: O(N) recursion depth
roi.signals: Find 'all possible' ways, combinations, permutations, subsets, paths.

codeLines.text: private static void backtrack(int[] nums, int start, List<Integer> bag, List<List<Integer>> result) {
// STEP 1 — SAVE: Current bag state is a valid subset (even empty bag)
// STEP 2 — LOOP: Try including each remaining item
for (int i = start; i < nums.length; i++) {
// STEP 3 — CHOOSE: Add item to the bag
// STEP 4 — EXPLORE: Go deeper (only items AFTER i to avoid duplicates)
// STEP 5 — UNCHOOSE (BACKTRACK): Remove item and try skipping it

traceSteps.title: STEP 1 — SAVE
traceSteps.text: Current bag state is a valid subset (even empty bag)

traceSteps.title: STEP 4 — EXPLORE
traceSteps.text: Go deeper (only items AFTER i to avoid duplicates)

traceSteps.title: STEP 5 — UNCHOOSE (BACKTRACK)
traceSteps.text: Remove item and try skipping it

sandbox.question: Why do we add a new ArrayList(bag) instead of just result.add(bag)?
options: Because bag is passed by reference, adding it directly would just add a reference to the same modifying list.

sandbox.question: What does the 'choose' step do in backtracking?
options: Adds the current element to our temporary combination before exploring further

sandbox.question: Why is the 'unchoose' (backtrack) step critical?
options: To return to the previous state so we can explore other valid choices at this level
